beats.why_it_mattered: Subtraction was the right launch-eve posture: every dead path removed is a failure mode that can't fire mid-cycle, and features added in a hurry are how launch days break. The guard rules are the substantive piece — in an environment where AI agents run the terminal, 'destructive cloud commands ask first' isn't bureaucracy, it's the blast-radius control that makes the autonomy defensible in the first place.
beats.gotcha: The continuous-integration pipeline only runs its lint and test gates on pushes to the main branch — not on the pull requests themselves — and its deploy step waits behind a manual approval click. So a code change can sit "green" on a PR having never been tested by CI at all; the real coverage came from each agent running the suite locally before merge, and the deploys went out by hand rather than through the gated pipeline.
beats.honest_miss: The hard problems were deliberately left untouched: the character-growth math that has to stay defensible over a year, the launch-day timeline that still contradicts itself, and several AI-quality gates all wait for the next session. The realistic-data blind spot in the pre-merge render check from the day before is filed but not fixed. And the health check still reports one failure — expected on a Day-0 site with almost no data yet, but a real red until the experiment actually starts.

beats.title: The character's math had to survive a year of adversarial scrutiny — so we simulated the year first
beats.shipped: A 420-day simulation harness drove the real character engine through five life trajectories — steady-good, sustained excellence, oscillation, a 30-day silent month, a slow improver — and the numbers didn't survive: a realistic-good year bled a permanent debt badge (the slow improver ended with 0 XP and 700 debt despite reaching level 57), an uninstrumented pillar accrued a phantom −100 forever, the top tier was mathematically unreachable at any horizon, and a fully silent month cost the headline level about 2 points — the exact failure that triggered last cycle's truth audit. One recalibration shipped the fix wave whole: the XP zero-point moved from 'a near-perfect day' to 'a decent day' so debt is something a recovery arc visibly repays; a confirmed dark stretch now keeps pulling the headline down day after day instead of re-arming a fresh grace window per drop (the silent month costs 12 visible levels and leaves a scar); pillars with no data source neither earn nor owe; and the two side doors that skipped every honesty gate — a food-delivery penalty and challenge bonus XP — now enter through the engine with provenance. Lifting, reading, and the task queue finally feed the sheet too, all as day-counts so junk volume buys nothing. Alongside it, nine more backlog items merged and deployed the same session, including a launch-eve timeline that contradicted itself about what day it was.
beats.why_it_mattered: The character sheet is a year-long public claim, and the gamification only works if a good year feels good: math that hands a steadily-improving person a permanent debt badge would collapse the metaphor and the motivation with it. Simulating 420 days before living them found failure modes no unit test could see — including the retune's own brand-new bug — and left the simulation standing as the regression gate for every future tuning argument.
beats.gotcha: The first simulation run after the retune showed the silent month costing NOTHING — the healthier XP economy kept every pillar's demotion buffer pinned at its cap, silently granting about 40 days of immunity. A unit test would never have seen it; the year-scale sim caught it in one run. The buffer is now capped at 40 and a provable absence bypasses it entirely.
beats.honest_miss: The tuning was ratified by simulation, not by lived data — cycle 5 starts tomorrow, and the first weeks of real scores may still argue with the curves. The sim harness is now the regression gate for exactly that conversation.

beats.title: Hours before Day 1, the cockpit got the two stations it was missing
beats.shipped: The morning page showed what happened yesterday but not what's actually in play today — so the loop the home page teaches never closed on the one screen that opens every morning. Eleven pull requests later it does: a levers station lists the supplement stack and any running experiment as 'day N of D' with a progress meter, and an inputs station shows a freshness mark per manual channel — food, journal, training — framed as instrument health, never a streak (the channel list comes from the same registry the pipeline uses, so it can't drift). The silence that quietly ended the last cycle would now be visible on this page by day two. Around those two stations: the deepest pages (a labs readout ~19 screens tall on a phone) got a sticky on-this-page bar with shareable section links, the topic rails got real anchors and a position readout instead of blind swiping, slow fetches got skeletons instead of six seconds of bare text, the service worker's footprint became a decision instead of an accident, and the public ask-the-board surface now runs behind the same coach-voice quality gate as the daily brief — fail-open under a strict time budget, so voice checking can never become a reader-facing timeout.
beats.why_it_mattered: The cockpit opens every morning, but it only showed yesterday — the loop the home page teaches never closed on the one screen that matters most. The inputs station is the real insurance: the last cycle ended through quiet manual-channel silence that took two weeks to notice, and that same silence would now be visible on this page by day two. Instrument health, never streaks — the framing is the honesty.
beats.gotcha: Two of the site deploys failed before a single page shipped: the asset pipeline builds a dependency graph by scanning for asset paths, and a stylesheet's header comment politely crediting its companion script read as a circular import. The deploy died safely — the live site never noticed — but the fix was rewording a comment, and the pipeline now carries a warning about the trap.
beats.honest_miss: The training lever isn't on the levers station yet — the platform holds the routine data but serves no endpoint for it, so that lever shipped as a filed follow-up instead of a feature. And the new Whoop re-auth script's interactive browser leg is untested until the next real token expiry forces the drill.

beats.title: Genesis night: the cockpit's last lever, and a day of silently red CI
beats.shipped: Hours into Day 1, the levers station got the third row it shipped without: the current training block, backed by a new read-only endpoint that deliberately returns counts only — six exercises, eighteen sets — never exercise names, loads, or private notes. The same sweep resolved a trust contradiction (the privacy page now scopes affiliate arrangements to the gear page's disclosed links, the owner's call) and gave the career essay a measurement instrument: the weekly traffic digest now reports that page's views and external referrers, so if it travels, the travel is on the record. Four pull requests, all merged and live.
beats.why_it_mattered: The counts-only endpoint is a privacy decision made visible: the training block's shape is public, the sets and loads are not, and the API physically can't serve what the page shouldn't show. The CI find is the durable lesson — a pipeline can sit red for 26 hours when it's not the one anybody watches, so green on the visible pipeline is not green. And instrumenting the essay's traffic means the career artifact gets measured, not assumed.
beats.gotcha: The first merge of the night surfaced that the main CI pipeline had been red for about 26 hours without anyone noticing. A cleanup the previous day archived a one-time migration script; a test still imported it from the old path. Nobody saw it because the site deploys through a separate workflow that stayed green — the pipeline that was failing is the one nobody was watching. The fix was one line; the reflex — check the other pipeline's conclusions at every close — is the real ship.
beats.honest_miss: The training lever currently renders an honest placeholder — 'staged for Day 1' — because the first cycle-5 training block hasn't been drafted yet; the row proves the plumbing, not the plan. And a scary-looking token failure from the scale vendor turned out to be transient after diagnosis, but its dead-letter message is still sitting in the queue awaiting a human hand — the automation could diagnose it, but deleting production state stays behind a human approval it didn't get in time.

beats.title: The site learned what day it is
beats.shipped: Matthew walked the whole site the day before the restart and wrote down 41 things that felt wrong — coaches quoting data from the previous attempt, a steps average built from a single partial day, discoveries listed before Day 1. Every finding was traced to its actual cause, filed, and fixed in 18 merged pull requests: every AI narrator now receives the experiment's day number before it writes a word, an AI reviewer now reads each page nightly as a first-time reader who knows what day it is, the restart collapsed from eight manual steps to one command with a truth-check gate, and the daily instrument moved to /cockpit/ — its real name.
beats.why_it_mattered: Under a dozen surface bugs was one root cause: no AI narrator knew what day it was, so the machine performed continuity instead of having it. Feeding every prompt the experiment's real phase — and hiring a nightly reader who checks each page against that truth — fixes the class, not the instances. And the four confident fixes killed by verification were the cheapest work of the session: wrong fixes compound, dropped ones don't.
beats.gotcha: Half the 'bugs' weren't bugs. The 'sixteen attempts' line one reviewer flagged as a hallucination is true biography; the time-travel slider reaching back through old cycles was a designed feature; two 'leaked' numbers traced to a single database row that a warm server had stamped with stale constants after the reset ran. An adversarial verification pass killed four confident fixes that would each have made things worse — the cheapest work of the whole session.
beats.honest_miss: The quality gates themselves were phase-blind: the vision QA was explicitly told to judge pixels and never truth, and the deploy smoke test demanded a body weight on a morning when none could honestly exist yet. Both are fixed, but they shipped that way — and the richer work the review asked for (immersive coach bios, badge pages, deeper protocol stories) is filed, not built.

beats.title: The machine audited its own story — and failed itself on purpose
beats.shipped: The evening of Day 1, the remaining backlog got burned down: 17 issues and 7 epics closed across 21 merged pull requests. Two drills anchored it. The disaster-recovery drill restored the entire database to an isolated copy in 4 minutes 32 seconds, then proved the documented recovery plan was broken as written — permissions are pinned to the exact production table, so the 'just repoint the fleet' step would have been denied mid-crisis; the cutover is now one command, measured at roughly 15 minutes against the 30 the docs had asserted on faith. The reset drill ran the new AI truth-reader against the live site, and it failed the build honestly: the homepage promised 'every day, including the ones that dipped' above a chart holding exactly one dot. Fixed, re-verified, and the drill is now a required step before any future restart. Around the drills: every supplement, experiment, and challenge now states what it hopes to change and how that gets measured, the head coach finally got his own page, coach bios became live dossiers showing the actual prompt material each AI voice is built from, and a generated rulebook at /method/game/ explains the character system from its real constants — with a tripwire that reddens the build if the engine changes and the prose doesn't.
beats.why_it_mattered: A recovery plan that has never been executed is a hope, not a plan — this one turned out to be wrong in a way only a drill could expose, and the fix made it wrong-proof rather than better-documented. Same logic on the truth gate: its first real run catching the site in a lie is the system working, because a checker that has never failed anything is indistinguishable from a checker that checks nothing.
beats.gotcha: The deploy pipeline rolled the site back twice for a bug that didn't exist: the CDN had cached a 404 from the sixty-second window before the new coach route finished deploying, and served that stale error to the smoke test long after the route was healthy. The fix was cache invalidation, not code. Separately, a one-line truth fix shipped a syntax error that the local checker waved through — JavaScript's engine defers some checks inside function bodies, so only the pipeline's stricter full parse caught it. The publish gate blocked it before readers ever saw a broken page, which is the gate doing its job.
beats.honest_miss: The truth-reader still flags the pre-rendered placeholder text it sees before the live numbers bind in — a first-time reader with JavaScript sees the real page, but the auditor reads the skeleton. Its verdicts get judged against the rendered page by hand for now. And the hypothesis statements now attached to 18 supplements were drafted by the machine from the evidence it holds; Matthew hasn't reviewed them yet, and they're flagged as awaiting exactly that.

beats.title: The gate said no fifteen times — and the epics only closed on evidence
beats.shipped: A late-evening close-out pass put six backlog epics in front of parallel verifiers, each checking the epic's own definition of done against the live site, real workflow history, and real AWS state — not just whether the child tickets were closed. Five closed on evidence; the mobile constellation got its type floored at a measured 11px effective with proper touch targets (live the same evening); and the drift sentinel — the weekly job that checks live infrastructure against code — got the IAM permissions it had silently lacked since it shipped, fixed in both sources of truth and proven with policy simulation.
beats.why_it_mattered: Closing an epic because its tickets are closed is bookkeeping; closing it because the live system demonstrably does what the epic promised is the honesty bar this platform sells. The sweep's two refusals are the point: one epic's headline number (30 consecutive green CI runs) has never actually been met, and the infrastructure-drift watchdog had never once run clean — its output bucket was empty, forever, behind a green checkmark. A watchdog that can't write its own report is worse than none, because it reads as coverage. Both defects are now fixed or on a dated path to close, in public.
beats.gotcha: The drift sentinel was starved by its own safety net. Its IAM permissions were written months ago but never applied to the live role — and then a later effort to codify roles into version control snapshotted the broken live state as the new canonical truth. From that moment the parity checker reported CLEAN on every run while the sentinel got AccessDenied on four of its seven checks. A parity check asserts 'repo matches live'; it cannot notice that both are wrong. The fix now verifies the capability itself with IAM policy simulation, not just the diff.
beats.honest_miss: The podcast's episode-0 regeneration failed its own quality gate fifteen times out of fifteen — across three campaigns and two rounds of increasingly explicit prompt rules, the model kept writing consecutive same-speaker turns even under a literal 'never two consecutive turns for the same speaker' instruction. The flawed live episode stays up until the fix moves from prompt to code, because the gate refusing to ship is the system working. Two epics also refused closure: the 30-green-streak criterion sits at a best-ever streak of 7, stated plainly on the issue rather than quietly waived.

beats.title: The podcast that refused to be boring — a gate that blocks flat, not just broken
beats.shipped: The show's Episode 0 got rebuilt end to end so it can never again ship a conversational hole where a dropped turn used to be — and, harder, so it can't pass as flat, AI-generated filler. The pipeline now repairs structural defects deterministically, runs a 'script doctor' punch-up pass that adds humour and warmth while the facts and turn order stay locked, and ends on a taste judge that has to quote the two funniest or most human lines it credits — or it fails the episode and holds. A new Episode 0 went live the same night, and it converged in one generation and one revision.
beats.why_it_mattered: The podcast is the flagship way a stranger meets this experiment, and the honest verdict on the old cut was that it was accurate but lifeless — nobody would keep listening. A quality gate that passes 'technically correct and boring' is worse than no gate, because it certifies the wrong thing. Now 'boring' is a fail condition the machine enforces on itself, with a real human line as the evidence it has to produce; and a bad episode escalates to one email for review rather than publishing or silently vanishing.
beats.gotcha: Fifteen straight regenerations failed the gate — and the reason turned out to be a law, not a bug: prompt instructions cannot guarantee structural properties of the output. The model kept writing two turns in a row for the same speaker even under an explicit, capitalized 'never do this' rule. The only fix that worked was to stop asking and start enforcing it in code: detect the seam, repair just that seam, then send the whole thing back through the unchanged gate.
beats.honest_miss: The audio identity isn't there yet. A synthesized musical bed sounded like a phone alarm, and a voice-only open was honest but bland — so the branded 'show open' is postponed until a real, properly-licensed music track gets chosen, and for now Episode 0 opens clean with no theme. Getting the words and the delivery right was reachable; getting music that sounds like a real show, in a sample-free codebase, wasn't — and pretending otherwise would have shipped the alarm.

beats.title: The board goes on the record — and I catch myself about to publish the wrong number
beats.shipped: Before Day 1 of the reset experiment, the AI coaching board filed its opening bets: 16 falsifiable predictions and 2 hypotheses, each one dated, frozen, and wired straight into the grading engine that will later mark them right or wrong. The public 'The Plan, On the Record' chapter went live alongside the restored lead-in chapter, so the story now opens where it should — with the plan committed in writing, in advance, where no one can quietly edit it after the fact.
beats.why_it_mattered: Pre-registration is the honesty mechanism of the whole experiment: you write down what you think will happen before it happens, so you can't cherry-pick a flattering story from the data later. A coaching board that only ever comments after the outcome is a horoscope. One that commits specific, gradeable predictions up front — and then gets scored on them in public — is accountable. Restoring it after the reset wasn't optional; it's the part that makes the rest trustworthy.
beats.gotcha: Re-generating the predictions for the new cycle, I ran a grep for stale numbers before publishing — and found the tool had hardcoded the OLD baseline weight (300.8 lb) in two places instead of reading it from the plan file. The reset had already changed the real starting weight to 314 lb. Publishing as-is would have carved the wrong number into a permanent, public, on-the-record document. The fix was to make it read the number from the plan every time, so a future reset can never reintroduce the same lie.
beats.honest_miss: The reset's own safety check cried wolf. The tool that scans the live site for stale dates flagged eight pages as 'leaking' — but the dates it caught were perfectly fresh real-time data that just happened to match the day the reset ran. The reset was correct; the checker was wrong, and its false alarm quietly skipped the step that publishes the chapters, which is why they were missing in the first place. Fixed the checker to tell a real leak apart from today's honest timestamp.

beats.title: 17 AI experts grade the platform — and their gate rolls back a healthy deploy for being honest
beats.shipped: The first full review of the whole platform: a panel of 17 specialist AI reviewers (architecture, security, accessibility, cost, narrative, a first-time reader, and twelve more) each graded their area and filed evidence-backed findings, then a second wave of adversarial verifiers tried to refute every single claim. 89 raw findings came back; 72 survived verification and every one is now a tracked issue with a fix path and a regression guard. No area graded below B. Along the way, the months-long invisible bug from last time — every social share card rendering as empty boxes — finally merged and went live.
beats.why_it_mattered: A self-built system accumulates blind spots the builder can't see, and a single reviewer inherits the builder's assumptions. Seventeen independent perspectives plus a refute-first verification pass is the closest a one-person operation gets to a real external audit — and the two biggest things it caught were systemic: pages quietly narrating the previous experiment cycle after a reset, and monitoring that reports success while silently broken. Both are now epics with every instance enumerated.
beats.gotcha: Mid-session, the site's own accuracy gate auto-rolled-back a perfectly healthy deploy. Why? Current weight is 2 lb above the new cycle's baseline, so 'progress toward goal' is legitimately negative — and the gate had hard-coded that a percentage below zero is impossible. The honesty the platform is built on tripped the alarm built to protect it. The fix teaches the gate that an honest bad week is in range.
beats.honest_miss: 17 of the 89 findings (19%) were false positives the verifiers had to kill — better than the historical ~50%, but still a fifth of the panel's output. And the weakest grades landed on the story surfaces themselves: the narrative and observability areas both graded B, which is the review saying the site's storytelling and its self-monitoring lag the engineering underneath.

beats.title: The public cost page was quoting a budget ceiling we retired two amendments ago
beats.shipped: A batch of eight fixes from the platform's first full review shipped and went live. The headline one: the public 'inference receipt' — the page that shows exactly what the AI costs — had a hard-coded $75 monthly ceiling baked into it, but the real ceiling is $85 and floats to $100 when reader traffic surges (which it currently is). The page now reads the live number straight from the budget governor, so it can never drift again. The same batch also fixed shared links that 404'd: typing or pasting a bare address like the site's /data or /cockpit page (many link tools strip the trailing slash) used to land on a dead page; now it redirects cleanly to the real one. Plus a cost-alert email that named the wrong features as paused, a chronicle draft that called July 13th a Sunday (it was a Monday), and a monitoring permission that was missing so a health metric never reported.
beats.why_it_mattered: The whole premise of this project is that the numbers are real — so a flagship 'here's what it costs' page showing a figure that's $25 stale is exactly the kind of quiet lie the platform exists to not tell. Deriving it from the one source of truth means the honesty is structural, not a thing someone has to remember to update. The routing fix matters for a different reason: it's the difference between a shared link working and a stranger's first impression being a broken page.
beats.gotcha: The routing fix had a tail: the new rule adds a trailing slash to any bare address, which is exactly the point — but it also means a genuinely non-existent page now gets one redirect before its 404, and the site's own smoke test asserted a direct 404. So a correct fix turned a health check red. The right fix wasn't to weaken the redirect but to teach the test to check the final destination, not the first hop.
beats.honest_miss: Two follow-ups are deliberately left undone. A monitoring alarm that had been firing because a permission was missing now has the permission — but it's still red, honestly this time: no prediction has been graded in 8 days because the current experiment cycle is only days old and nothing is due yet. And two historical-data cleanups (re-stamping some old archived records, regenerating one held draft) weren't run — healing past data by hand is riskier than the code fix that stops it recurring, so those wait for a careful pass.

beats.title: The home page had to survive mental arithmetic
beats.shipped: A batch of reader-facing honesty fixes went live across the home and coaching doors. The hero's weight line now reconciles — it shows 315.6 lb everywhere and the 1.6 lb delta matches what you can subtract yourself, instead of showing a rounded 316 next to a 1.6 that didn't add up. A single weigh-in now reads "up 1.6 lb since the start — one weigh-in so far" rather than inventing a multi-day trend. Each coach's read is stamped with the date it was written. The waveform stops rendering a 2.8% dip as an 86% collapse. And a new gate flags a verdict like "strong recovery" when the number underneath (44%) sits in the yellow band.
beats.why_it_mattered: The site's whole claim is proof, not promises — so the very first number a reader checks has to hold. A hero stat where 316 minus 314 doesn't equal the 1.6 shown next to it, or a coach quoting Day-1 vitals with no date one click from a cockpit showing today's, teaches a numerate reader to discount every later figure. A reader who catches one inflated label discounts all of them; these fixes are about keeping that trust intact, not about any measured reaction.
beats.gotcha: The site's auto-deploy lied by omission. Each fix merged clean and the "Site deploy" run reported success — but a "superseded-run" check had silently skipped the actual publish every time, because a bookkeeping commit landed on the branch a few seconds after each merge and made the deploy think a newer run would handle it. Nothing did. The give-away was the live build stamp still pointing at the pre-fix version; a browser render check caught readers being served the old assets. The fix was to deploy the site by hand and stop trusting a green checkmark that published nothing.
beats.honest_miss: The "strong recovery at a yellow number" gate only covers recovery percentage — HRV and sleep don't yet have a clean, personally-calibrated band to check an adjective against, so those verdicts still pass unexamined. And the fixes to already-written coach summaries and chronicle excerpts only take effect the next time those are regenerated; the copy sitting in storage from before the fix stays as-is until then.

beats.title: The scoreboard that grades our forecasts was showing zero
beats.shipped: Seventeen backlog fixes went live in one sitting, and the one that stung most was the calibration page. It reads the platform's own graded predictions and reports how well-calibrated they are — and it was showing n=0, no data, while the forecast engine had already graded 23 interval forecasts at 82.6% coverage sitting right next to it. The two lived in the same ledger; the scoreboard just dropped every row that graded itself by whether the interval held ("covered") instead of by a yes/no "outcome" word. Now the page reports all 23. In the same batch: the home share-card stopped boasting "25 data sources" when there are 16 (it now counts the registry itself, so it can't drift again), the experiment library shed four copy-paste near-duplicates (71 → 67 distinct pre-registered designs), the RSS feed stopped cutting descriptions mid-word, the cost-governor cadence doc stopped saying "hourly" when it runs every 8 hours, and the mobile waveform's day-bars finally reach a real 44px tap target.
beats.why_it_mattered: This site's entire claim is proof over promises, and the calibration page is where that claim is most literal — it publicly grades how often the predictions came true. A grading page that shows "no data" while 23 graded forecasts sit one endpoint away doesn't just look empty; it undercounts the very honesty record it exists to display. Every fix in the batch carried a regression test written to fail against the old code first, so the specific defect can't quietly return — that's the point, not any reaction to it.
beats.gotcha: The experiment-library dedupe deployed, the page showed the new count — and the public /api/experiments endpoint kept insisting there were 71. The site's auto-deploy publishes the site/ folder, but that API reads a catalog from a different root config/ object it never touches. The count was correct in two places and stale in the one a machine would actually query. The fix was to push that root object by hand and clear the cached response; the lesson is that "the page looks right" and "the API is right" are two separate deploys.
beats.honest_miss: The RSS feed still shows one chronicle excerpt cut mid-sentence — that copy was truncated and stored before the fix, so it stays wrong until the chronicle regenerates; only new posts get the clean cut. And the new fabricated-date check is wired into the weekly chronicle's grounding gate but not yet into the other narrative surfaces, so a made-up date is caught in one place, not everywhere.

beats.title: The archive got its memory back — then we reset the whole experiment for Sunday
beats.shipped: The backlog's tail got drained and the archive got healed in one sitting. The cockpit now says plainly that its daily line is yesterday's read (it sat one inch above a live panel showing a different HRV number with no scope label), carried-forward protocol dates say "carried from prep" instead of looking stale, and the home page grew a season-premiere beat comparing the new start to every start before it — self-hiding after day 21. Under the hood, the biggest file in the repo (4,184 lines, 37 API handlers) was split along domain seams with route-for-route parity proven, and 8,427 archived records got their true cycle stamps back: every past reset had been re-stamping the whole archive with the newest cycle number, so a record from April claimed it was from July. Then, healed archive in hand, the experiment reset one more time — cycle 8, genesis Sunday July 19.
beats.why_it_mattered: Two kinds of honesty were on the line. Reader-facing: two true numbers that look contradictory without a scope label read as a bug, and a pre-genesis date without a "carried" marker reads as neglect — both are trust leaks on the highest-traffic page. Archive-facing: the whole point of stamping archived records by reset generation is answering "which run did this belong to?", and the stamps were all lies. The heal used each record's own content date against the authoritative cycle ledger, and the wipe was fixed so future resets can never overwrite a stamp again — cycle 8's reset ran over the healed archive and left it intact, which was the point of doing them in that order.
beats.gotcha: The first dry-run of the archive heal would have made everything worse: it inferred each record's era from its tombstone timestamp — which is the timestamp of the reset that buried it, the exact corruption being healed. 8,000+ rows would have been re-stamped toward the reset dates with authoritative-looking wrongness. The dry-run caught it before a single write. Separately, a one-line CSS class that passed the browser-level visual QA still turned the main branch red an hour later: a unit-level design-token guard rejects raw font sizes, and no amount of pixel-perfect rendering satisfies it.
beats.honest_miss: The 198 pilot-era records from before cycle 1 were left unstamped on purpose — they predate the numbering and forcing them into it would be its own small lie. 2,243 undateable singleton records also keep their wrong stamps; they rebuild themselves on the next compute. And the coach portrait redesign got as far as a rendered option round (four ways to stop the frame reading as a clock) but no decision — only Matthew approves portrait art, and that pick is deliberately still open.

beats.title: The site stopped contradicting itself — and the restarts became the story
beats.shipped: Two same-morning surfaces disagreed about the same body: one page said recovery 96%, another said 0.0% and colored it red — because four different code paths each derived 'current vitals' their own way. Now ONE canonical resolver feeds every public surface (pulse, snapshot, the stats file the homepage reads), a contract test fails CI if two pages ever disagree again, and the platform counts on the site derive from the code registries instead of three hand-typed numbers that had drifted years apart in spirit. Same honesty pass elsewhere: a forecaster scoring worse than the base rate can no longer wear a 'Well Calibrated' badge (it now reads 'Not Yet Skillful'), and the wrong-page's header count finally matches the rows it renders. Then the biggest one: /story/attempts/ — the serial restarter's ledger. Every one of the 7 attempts on one day-axis chart, cause of death and all, with the cockpit masthead now reading 'Attempt #8 — previous best: 13 days' the day before cycle 8 arms. And the ops layer got quieter and truer: the public API's concurrency cap had been silently throttling readers with 429s for 21 of 30 days (cap 5, hit daily) — raised to 20 with real Throttles alarms; and the CI badge means something again, because one stale queue message had redded 38 consecutive runs until the check learned to date messages instead of counting them.
beats.gotcha: A manual deploy got silently overwritten two minutes later by the still-running CI of an OLDER merge — the runs for the newer commits had been auto-cancelled as 'superseded', but the old one kept going and shipped its stale tree. Live behavior reverted to pre-fix while every deploy log said success. The fix to trust: read the deployed artifact's hash, never the deploy script's checkmark.
beats.honest_miss: The new attempts chart shipped with a comment claiming its tiny SVG text was 'floored at 11px effective' — it wasn't; the floor mechanism was never wired, and on a phone the labels rendered at 5px. A browser-measuring QA pass caught the false claim (twice — fixing the floor then exposed label clipping the floor had been hiding). The sanction comment now has to be true, not just well-phrased.
beats.why_it_mattered: Honesty is the platform's entire moat, and it was failing at the two most visible points: the numbers (two pages, two answers) and the narrative (seven restarts read as shameful emptiness on every day-1). Structural truth — one resolver, derived counts, contract tests — makes the site agree with itself by construction, and the attempts ledger turns the most embarrassing fact into the frame that makes day 2 worth reading.

beats.title: Day 1 reads as an instrument arming, not a broken site
beats.shipped: Attempt #8 starts today, and the cold start finally tells the truth about itself. Every 'not enough data yet' threshold the engines actually enforce now lives in ONE registry the site reads back — so an empty correlations page renders the real trigger ('first pairs at n≥10 overlapping days — currently 0/10') as a row of hollow marks that fill as days accrue, instead of authored 'check back soon' copy. Months-old source chips on the pipeline board are now labeled 'carried from a previous attempt' with the cycle genesis decoded, instead of reading like outages. And the AI coaches carry a hard day-one rule: a gap the reset itself manufactured ('zero food logs' on the first morning) is the instrument arming, never a scolding. Same session, the quiet flourishing layer went live: the daily LLM-coded journal signals (values lived, gratitude, flow, social quality) became a first-class queryable series with model provenance on every row — and two character pillars now draw from it.
beats.gotcha: A refactor made one database write's destination dynamic — and the CI gate that proves every partition the site reads has a writer could no longer see it, redding two pipelines. Locally the failure hid behind pytest -x stopping at an unrelated stale generated page. Fix: write destinations stay inline literals, and full suites run without -x before a merge queue.
beats.honest_miss: The 'carried from attempt N' chips mostly can't name N yet — raw sensor records never got cycle stamps, and the serving role can't read the cycle parameter — so they fall back to 'a previous attempt'. Honest, but vaguer than designed.
beats.why_it_mattered: A restart is the platform's most dangerous moment for trust — the morning the numbers vanish is exactly when a reader decides whether the honesty was real. Making the empty states computed, provenance-labeled, and unscolding is what lets attempt #8 open in public without the site lying about its own blank spots.

beats.title: Day 1 opened with a 44-issue overnight drain — and the instrument grading itself
beats.shipped: The night before Day 1 of cycle 8, an overnight multi-agent session closed 44 backlog issues (154 open → 110), every one shipped with a regression guard proven to fail on the pre-fix tree, merged, deployed, and live-verified. The headliners: ONE page registry now drives every QA sweep (the four hand-maintained page lists are dead — 79 of 80 live pages in the Playwright sweep, up from 36); the character sheet earned a felt-reality calibration card (a 20-second Sunday probe pairs how the week actually felt against each pillar's level — served honestly as 'uncalibrated, 0 of 5 weeks' until the data exists); the crawler view came alive (real numbers in every page's noscript static core + data-driven OG tags — shares stop unfurling blank); career-vs-season stats survived the reset (lifetime n beside the fresh cycle's zero, sports-card style); and the design-partner pipeline (bundle builder, /design-sync, /design-implement, the anti-slop brief) went end-to-end buildable.
beats.gotcha: Genesis eve itself found a bug: /api/fulfillment_ritual 500'd for four hours because a date clamp used UTC while its callers used Pacific — a seven-hour window that inverts a DynamoDB BETWEEN every single reset eve. Fixed the same night, with the incident logged and a regression test pinned to the Pacific contract.
beats.honest_miss: The production deploy approval gate documented in four places turned out not to exist — and the root cause was nobody's delete: flipping the repo private silently dropped the protection rule because the billing plan doesn't support it on private repos. Two auto-rollbacks also fired on HEALTHY deploys (a deploy-ordering race and a CloudFront cache race) — the safety net works, but twice tonight it worked against us; both classes now have fixes or filed hardening.
beats.why_it_mattered: An experiment that restarts eight times lives or dies on how cheap and honest a restart is. Tonight the machine drained a quarter of its own backlog in one sitting while the instrument learned to grade itself against felt reality — and every close had to prove its guard failed before the fix existed. That discipline is the difference between a backlog that shrinks and a count that's theater.
